keyFacts: Tallest eastern hardwood, reaching 150+ feet.
Distinctive tulip-shaped leaves with four lobes and a flat or slightly notched tip.
Yellow-green tulip-shaped flowers appear in late spring.
habitat: Rich, moist soils of coves and lower slopes in the Blue Ridge.
identificationTips: Look for the unique four-lobed leaf shape — no other eastern tree has it.

keyFacts: Five needles per bundle (mnemonic: W-H-I-T-E has 5 letters).
Tallest native conifer in eastern North America.
Soft, flexible needles 2-5 inches long.
habitat: Moist, well-drained soils; coves and ridges.
identificationTips: Count the needles in each bundle — 5 means white pine.

keyFacts: One of the first trees to flower in spring with small red blossoms.
Leaves have three main lobes with serrated margins.
Brilliant red fall color gives this tree its name.
habitat: Adaptable to wet or dry soils; found throughout the eastern forest.
identificationTips: Three-lobed leaves with V-shaped sinuses (vs. sugar maple's U-shaped).

keyFacts: Short, flat needles with two white stripes on the underside.
Tiny cones only 3/4 inch long.
Threatened by hemlock woolly adelgid, an invasive insect.
habitat: Cool, moist ravines and north-facing slopes; often along streams.
identificationTips: Flat needles attached by a tiny stem; small cones hanging from branch tips.

keyFacts: Leaves have rounded lobes without bristle tips.
Can live 600+ years; one of the most important hardwoods in eastern forests.
Acorns mature in one season and are less bitter than red oak group.
habitat: Well-drained upland soils; mixed hardwood forests.
identificationTips: Rounded leaf lobes (vs. pointed lobes in red oak group); light gray flaky bark.

keyFacts: Evergreen fern; stays green through winter (hence 'Christmas').
Individual leaflets shaped like a Christmas stocking.
One of the most common ferns in eastern North American forests.
habitat: Shaded slopes, stream banks, and rocky woods.
identificationTips: Look for the 'ear' or 'thumb' at the base of each leaflet — shaped like a stocking.

keyFacts: Distinctive hooded flower (spathe) sheltering a club-shaped spadix.
Can change sex from year to year depending on stored energy.
Produces bright red berry clusters in fall.
habitat: Rich, moist deciduous woods and floodplains.
identificationTips: Unmistakable hooded flower structure; compound leaves with three leaflets.

keyFacts: One of the earliest spring wildflowers, blooming March-April.
Stem and root exude bright orange-red sap when broken.
Single white flower with 8-12 petals wrapping around a single basal leaf.
habitat: Rich deciduous woods, often on slopes and near streams.
identificationTips: White flower emerges wrapped in a large, lobed leaf; orange sap in stem.

keyFacts: Heart-shaped velvety leaves growing low to the ground.
Hidden brownish-purple flower at ground level between two leaf stalks.
Root has a ginger-like fragrance (not related to culinary ginger).
habitat: Rich, moist deciduous forests in partial to full shade.
identificationTips: Paired heart-shaped fuzzy leaves; crush a leaf for the ginger scent.

keyFacts: Everything in threes: three leaves, three petals, three sepals.
Large white flowers turn pink as they age.
Takes 7-10 years to reach flowering maturity from seed.
habitat: Rich, mesic deciduous forests, especially cove hardwoods.
identificationTips: The 'rule of three' — if all parts come in threes, it's a trillium.

keyFacts: Loud, ringing 'teakettle-teakettle-teakettle' song.
Warm brown above, buffy below, with a bold white eyebrow stripe.
Pairs bond for life and often roost together in winter.
habitat: Dense underbrush, thickets, and forest edges.
identificationTips: Bold white eyebrow stripe; upturned tail; loud song disproportionate to small size.

keyFacts: Distinctive 'drink-your-tea' song.
Males have black head and back, rufous sides, white belly.
Scratches the ground with both feet simultaneously to find food.
habitat: Dense shrubby areas, forest edges, and overgrown fields.
identificationTips: Large sparrow with bold black, rufous, and white coloring; red eye in eastern birds.

keyFacts: One of the most common songbirds in eastern forests — sings nearly constantly.
Can sing over 20,000 songs per day.
Olive-green back, white below, with gray crown and white eyebrow bordered by black.
habitat: Deciduous and mixed forest canopy.
identificationTips: Plain olive-green bird that sings persistent, robin-like phrases from the canopy.

keyFacts: Considered to have one of the most beautiful songs of any North American bird.
Warm brown above with bold dark spots on white breast.
Populations declining due to habitat fragmentation and cowbird parasitism.
habitat: Interior of mature deciduous and mixed forests.
identificationTips: Pot-bellied thrush with bold, round breast spots (not streaks); flute-like song at dusk.

keyFacts: Breeding male is brilliant red with jet-black wings and tail.
Song sounds like a robin with a sore throat.
Females and non-breeding males are olive-yellow with darker wings.
habitat: Canopy of mature deciduous forests, especially oak-dominated stands.
identificationTips: Male's red body with black wings is unmistakable; listen for hoarse robin-like song.

keyFacts: Multicolored concentric zones on the upper surface resemble a wild turkey's tail.
One of the most common bracket fungi in the world.
Contains compounds (PSK, PSP) studied for immune-boosting properties.
habitat: Dead and decaying hardwood logs, stumps, and branches.
identificationTips: Fan-shaped brackets with concentric color bands; white pore surface underneath (not gills).

keyFacts: Bright orange and yellow shelf fungus that is a popular edible.
Grows in large overlapping clusters that can weigh over 50 pounds.
Causes brown rot in hardwood trees, especially oaks.
habitat: Living and dead hardwoods, especially oaks; occasionally on conifers.
identificationTips: Unmistakable bright orange-yellow shelves; soft and moist when young.

keyFacts: Prized edible mushroom with a fruity, apricot-like aroma.
Forms mycorrhizal relationships with oaks and other hardwoods.
Golden-yellow color throughout cap, false gills, and stem.
habitat: Oak and hardwood forests on well-drained slopes.
identificationTips: Egg-yolk yellow; blunt, forking ridges (not true gills) running down the stem.

keyFacts: Shiny, varnished-looking reddish-brown cap is distinctive.
Grows primarily on eastern hemlock (Tsuga canadensis).
Used in traditional medicine for centuries; closely related to Ganoderma lucidum.
habitat: Dead and dying hemlocks, occasionally other conifers.
identificationTips: Kidney-shaped, glossy reddish-brown cap; white pore surface; woody texture.

keyFacts: Common bracket fungus with a distinctive violet-purple pore surface when fresh.
One of the first fungi to colonize dead hardwood.
Upper surface is grayish-white with faint zones.
habitat: Dead hardwood logs, stumps, and branches.
identificationTips: Look for the purple-violet color on the underside pore surface, especially on fresh specimens.

keyFacts: Most common large mammal in eastern North America.
Named for the white underside of the tail, flashed as an alarm signal.
Bucks grow and shed antlers annually; antlers can span over 2 feet.
habitat: Forest edges, meadows, and mixed woodlands.
identificationTips: Large brown deer; white tail flashes when running; bucks have branching antlers.

keyFacts: Bushy tail used for balance, communication, and warmth.
Buries thousands of nuts each fall but forgets many, helping plant trees.
Can rotate hind ankles 180 degrees to descend trees headfirst.
habitat: Hardwood and mixed forests, parks, and suburban areas.
identificationTips: Gray fur with white belly; large bushy tail; active during the day.

keyFacts: Five dark stripes alternating with lighter stripes along the back.
Cheek pouches can expand to three times the size of the head.
Builds extensive underground burrow systems up to 30 feet long.
habitat: Deciduous forests with rocky areas, stone walls, and log piles.
identificationTips: Small, striped ground squirrel; loud, repeated 'chip' call; cheeks often stuffed with food.

keyFacts: Western NC has one of the densest black bear populations in the Southeast.
Omnivorous — diet is 85% plant material (berries, nuts, grasses).
Can run up to 35 mph and are excellent tree climbers.
habitat: Mature forests with dense understory; often near streams and berry patches.
identificationTips: Solid black fur (occasionally brown muzzle); no shoulder hump (vs. grizzly); straight face profile.

keyFacts: Distinctive black mask across the eyes and ringed tail.
Extremely dexterous front paws used to manipulate food and open containers.
Highly adaptable; thrives in forests, wetlands, and urban areas.
habitat: Forests near water, wetlands, and suburban areas.
identificationTips: Black facial mask; bushy ringed tail; stocky body; active at night.

keyFacts: One of the most recognizable butterflies in eastern North America.
Males are yellow with black tiger stripes; females can be yellow or dark morph.
Wingspan of 3.5 to 5.5 inches — one of the largest eastern butterflies.
habitat: Forest edges, gardens, meadows, and near streams.
identificationTips: Large yellow butterfly with bold black stripes and tail-like extensions on hindwings.

keyFacts: Famous for annual migration of up to 3,000 miles to central Mexico.
Caterpillars feed exclusively on milkweed, making adults toxic to predators.
Orange wings with black veins and white-spotted black borders.
habitat: Open meadows, fields, and gardens with milkweed.
identificationTips: Orange wings with black veins; white dots along black wing borders; slow, gliding flight.

keyFacts: Drills perfectly round holes in wood to create nesting galleries.
Males are territorial but cannot sting; females rarely sting.
Important native pollinator — larger and more efficient than honeybees for some plants.
habitat: Forest edges, gardens, and near wooden structures.
identificationTips: Large, robust bee; shiny black abdomen (vs. fuzzy bumblebee); hovers near wood surfaces.

keyFacts: Striking lime-green wings with long, twisting tails on the hindwings.
Adults have no mouth and do not eat — they live only about one week.
Wingspan up to 4.5 inches; one of the largest moths in North America.
habitat: Deciduous and mixed forests; attracted to lights at night.
identificationTips: Unmistakable large pale green moth with long hindwing tails and eyespots on each wing.

keyFacts: Males have entirely jet-black wings and metallic green bodies.
Females have smoky wings with a white spot near the tip (stigma).
Flutter-like flight is unique among damselflies — looks almost butterfly-like.
habitat: Shaded, clean forest streams and small rivers.
identificationTips: Damselfly with broad, dark wings; iridescent green body; slow, fluttery flight along streams.

keyFacts: Can close its shell completely using a hinged plastron — like a box.
Can live over 100 years in the wild.
Omnivorous, eating mushrooms, berries, insects, and snails.
habitat: Moist deciduous forests, meadow edges, and floodplains.
identificationTips: High-domed shell with variable orange, yellow, and brown patterns; hinged bottom shell.

keyFacts: Juveniles have bright blue tails that distract predators.
Five light stripes run along a dark body from head to tail.
Males develop orange-red heads during breeding season.
habitat: Rocky woodlands, rotting logs, and forest edges.
identificationTips: Sleek lizard with five cream stripes; juveniles have vivid blue tails; fast-moving.

keyFacts: One of the longest snakes in eastern North America — up to 6 feet.
Excellent climber; can scale vertical tree trunks and walls.
Non-venomous constrictor that helps control rodent populations.
habitat: Forests, farmlands, rocky hillsides, and old buildings.
identificationTips: Large shiny black snake (adults); juveniles are gray with dark blotches; smooth, powerful body.

keyFacts: Males have bright blue patches on throat and belly.
Often seen basking on fence posts, rocks, and logs.
Rough, keeled scales give a spiny appearance.
habitat: Dry, open forests with rocky outcrops and fallen logs.
identificationTips: Gray-brown spiny lizard; males flash blue belly patches; often on sunny surfaces.

keyFacts: Venomous pit viper — responsible for most snakebites in the Southeast.
Distinctive hourglass-shaped crossbands on a copper-tan body.
Freeze response when threatened makes them easy to step on accidentally.
habitat: Rocky, wooded hillsides; forest edges; near streams and logs.
identificationTips: Hourglass-shaped tan and copper crossbands; triangular head; vertical pupils; heat-sensing pits.

keyFacts: Three life stages: aquatic larva, terrestrial red eft, and aquatic adult.
Red eft stage is bright orange-red with red spots — a warning of skin toxins.
Adults are olive-green with red spots bordered in black.
habitat: Ponds and slow streams (adults); moist forest floor (efts).
identificationTips: Efts are bright orange-red on land; adults are greenish with red spots in water.

keyFacts: Warty skin secretes bufotoxin that deters most predators.
Long, sustained trilling call lasts up to 30 seconds during breeding.
One female can lay up to 20,000 eggs in long strings.
habitat: Gardens, forests, meadows — anywhere with moist cover and insect prey.
identificationTips: Warty brown skin; one or two warts per dark spot on back; parotoid glands behind eyes.

keyFacts: Tiny frog (under 1.5 inches) with an incredibly loud, high-pitched peep.
Dark X-shaped marking on the back is diagnostic.
One of the first frogs to call in spring — a harbinger of the season.
habitat: Wooded wetlands, pond edges, and swamps; breeds in temporary pools.
identificationTips: Tiny tan frog with dark X on back; loud single 'peep' repeated every second.

keyFacts: Lungless salamander — breathes entirely through its skin.
Two color phases: 'redback' (red stripe) and 'leadback' (all dark).
Most abundant vertebrate in many eastern forests.
habitat: Under rocks, logs, and leaf litter in moist forests.
identificationTips: Small, slender salamander; straight-edged red or orange dorsal stripe (or all gray 'leadback').

keyFacts: Call sounds like a loose banjo string — a single plunking note.
Dorsolateral ridges run partway down the back (vs. full length in bullfrog).
Males have a tympanum (ear disc) larger than their eye.
habitat: Ponds, streams, springs, and wetlands with permanent water.
identificationTips: Green to brown frog with dorsolateral ridges that stop before the hind legs; large tympanum in males.
